content.subtitle: Uses
items: When we don't know who does/did something
When it's obvious who does/did something
When it's not important who does/did something

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Her handbag was snatched outside the shopping centre early on Sunday morning.
Having been introduced in 1974, the Consumer Protection Act governs all commercial transactions in the UK.
The Z900 was engineered with sports enthusiasts in mind, delivering exceptional performance on any terrain.
This radar detection system was pioneered during the Cold War by Soviet military engineers.
We were overwhelmed by the number of applicants responding to the job advertisement.
All visitors are requested to register their details at the main reception desk.

content.subtitle: Form
items: noun + verb in passive form + infinitive / perfect infinitive
There + verb in passive form + infinitive / perfect infinitive
It + verb in passive form + that clause

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Online retail is expected to represent the largest share of the country's commercial activity.
There are reported to have been a record number of wildfires in the region this summer.
It is thought that the new high-speed rail link will create significant job opportunities for local communities.

content.subtitle: Form
items: Some verbs (bring, buy, get, give, leave, lend, make, offer, owe, pass, pay, promise, refuse, send, show, take, teach, tell, write) can take two objects.

content.subtitle: Example
items: Active: Rachel sent the conference documents to Marcus.
Passive (indirect object as subject): Marcus was sent the conference documents (by Rachel).
Passive (direct object as subject): The conference documents were sent to Marcus (by Rachel).

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: With explain and suggest, the preposition cannot be omitted. They cannot be used with indirect object as subject.

content.subtitle: Tenses to avoid
content.description: The passive is not normally used with present perfect continuous, past perfect continuous, future continuous or future perfect continuous.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: ✓ Renovations to the museum wing will be in progress as the delegates arrive.
✓ By the end of this term, she will have been in training as a nurse for three years.
✓ Original sculptures from the collection have been on display in the gallery all month.
✓ By the time a solution was reached, the matter had been under consideration for quite a while.
✓ The new concert hall has been under construction for eighteen months now.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions we arrange for other people to do for us
Things we experience (usually negative and not intended)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Did you finally get your laptop repaired?
I heard that David had his wallet stolen at the concert.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Get is more informal than have.
Can use need, want, would like: I'd like those files printed by this afternoon.
Get sth done can also mean 'finish doing something': Let's head off once I've got the report finished.

sections.title: Causative: get sb to do / have sb do

content.subtitle: Form
items: Actions we make somebody/something do for us

content.subtitle: Example
items: Did you get Sofia to drive you all the way to Edinburgh?

content.subtitle: Form
items: Actions we make somebody/something start doing

content.subtitle: Example
items: Don't panic. We'll soon have your laptop running perfectly again.

sections.title: A: Correct the Passive Form
sections.instructions: If the verb in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Could you clarify when the research grant **was been approved**?
When the competition winner **was finally announced**, the audience erupted in applause.
**Has** your reservation at the conference hotel next month **confirmed** by the organising committee?
Every delegate who **was been invited** to the summit attended the opening session.
The ballpoint pen **invented** in 1938 by László Bíró, a Hungarian-Argentine journalist.
The proposal **has debated** in the Senate last Tuesday, but no agreement was reached.
By the time we arrive at the venue, the luggage **should have been unloaded**.
The ancient city **was be discovered** by archaeologists during routine excavations in the 1980s.
The charity gala **had been organised** months before, yet several issues still arose on the day.
After **be examined** by the specialist, she was permitted to leave the clinic.
We boarded the vessel and **were directing** to our quarters by the first officer.
The new library **is to be inaugurated** by a well-known scientist next Friday.

sections.title: B: Passive Verb Forms
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct passive form in each gap.

items.sentence: The missing documents ……………………. in the drawer of an old desk just yesterday.
When the outdoor theatre opens next month, the opening performance ……………………… will be A Midsummer Night's Dream.
Several questions …………………………… in Parliament about the company's environmental record.
We are all looking forward to ………………………… a private tour of the new science wing.
Consider that when these lines ……………………… , the printing press had only just been invented.
Once the bonfire ………………………… , the celebrations can properly begin.
Before ……………………………… backstage, each journalist had to sign a confidentiality agreement.
She had always hoped ………………………… at the station by a familiar face.
It ………………………………… absolutely plain to the committee that further delays were unacceptable.
Patrick says he has always hoped …………………………… as a pioneer in sustainable architecture.

sections.title: C: Impersonal Passive
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Deforestation is thought **to be caused / is caused** by both agricultural expansion and illegal logging.
There **are to be estimated / are estimated** to be approximately 8,700 species of birds worldwide.
It **is to be reported / has been reported** that severe flooding has affected several coastal villages.
The fugitive is believed **to use / to have used** a forged identity document to cross the border.
It **is to be calculated / is calculated** that roughly 70% of the human body consists of water.
Nearly 350 passengers **are being understood / are understood** to have survived the rail accident unharmed.
It is considered rude in many traditions **to be pointed / to point** directly at another person.
There **mean / were meant** to have been 40 boxes of tiles in the delivery, not 400!
**It / There** has been suggested that commuters who use public transport should receive tax incentives.
Your parcel is guaranteed **will be delivered / to be delivered** within five working days.

sections.title: D: Error Correction (Sydney Opera House Text)
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains one verb in the wrong form. Underline the incorrect word or phrase and rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: The Sydney Opera House, situated on the shores of Sydney Harbour, **has called** one of the most recognisable architectural landmarks in the modern world.
The peninsula on which the building stands **sold** to the city authorities at the turn of the twentieth century.
...designed to house world-class performances, **was named** after the surrounding harbour precinct...
...the main auditorium, which **has later renamed** the Concert Hall, can accommodate an audience of over 2,600.
...ensuring that theatregoers could **been transported** directly from the central business district by ferry in just minutes.
Two additional rehearsal spaces... **have been completed** before the complex officially opened its doors to the public.
...a dedicated outdoor stage **has been incorporated** into the design, provided that sufficient funds could be raised.

sections.title: E: Avoiding the Passive
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences describing each situation using the phrase given.

items.sentence: By the time she graduates, ......
items.answer: she will have been in training as a surgeon for five years.

items.sentence: The redesign of the city's transport network ......
items.answer: has been under consideration for several months.

items.sentence: Negotiations between the two parties ......
items.answer: have been in progress for more than three weeks.

items.sentence: The museum's newest acquisitions ......
items.answer: will be on display at the main hall this weekend.

items.sentence: The town's new sports complex ......
items.answer: appears to have been under construction for far too long.

sections.title: F: Causative Error Correction
sections.instructions: If the word or phrase in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: We ended up having it **replaced** twice!
Well, we got **delivered it** to our door on the very first morning.
We decided to have it **check** straight away...
Oh, I didn't know you'd **had them do** that.
We had my neighbour **to take** a look at it.
items.answer: had my neighbour take a look

items.sentence: Did you have the contractor **to come** to inspect it?
We got my cousin **to working** on it.
I bet he soon had **running it** properly.
...we still had to get the contractor **come** back out in the end...
You should get them **to offer** you a partial refund.

sections.title: G: Causative Structures
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence using the noun in bold and an appropriate causative form of the verb in bold.

items.sentence: If you are unsure about anything, ...... (your tutor / EXPLAIN) it to you again after the lecture.
...watching the puppy chase its own tail soon ...... (me / FEEL) much better.
Don't bother painting the fence yourself – ...... (a professional / DO) it.
...... (our equipment / DAMAGE) was absolutely infuriating because it meant we had to delay the entire project.
You won't develop your skills as quickly if you always ...... (others / HELP) you with your coursework.
...the comedian soon ...... (us / LAUGH) to his outrageous stories.
Don't expect ...... (me / DO) your tedious administrative work for you!
Have you ever thought about ...... (your shutters / PAINT) the same shade as your front door?
I ...... (Tom / SHOW) me how to play the opening chords, but I still couldn't quite master them.
Have you seen the documentary about tourists ...... (their pictures / TAKE) at famous landmarks?

sections.title: H: Key Word Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Use between three and eight words.

items.sentence:
They forced us to sit in the corridor for nearly half an hour! **KEPT**
...... in the corridor for nearly half an hour!

Although people regard Oliver as an authority on the subject, he remains open to new ideas. **DESPITE**
Despite ...... on the subject, Oliver remains open to new ideas.

We have received information that the director is planning an unexpected visit to the branch. **REPORTED**
The director ...... an unexpected visit to the branch.

The headteacher offered all the pupils additional revision materials. **PROVIDED**
All the pupils ...... the headteacher.
items.answer: were provided with additional revision materials by

items.sentence:
I think someone ought to go through these safety procedures with me. **GET**
I think I should get ...... me.
items.answer: someone to go through these safety procedures with

items.sentence:
Workers have been building the new convention centre for much longer than the council promised. **UNDER**
The new convention centre ...... for much longer than the council promised.

Do you know why the supervisor made Ellen work overtime on Friday? **MADE**
Do you know why ...... overtime on Friday by the supervisor?

Ring the receptionist and ask her to forward the documents to my office. **HAVE**
Ring the receptionist and ...... the documents to my office.
When Rosa appeared on stage, she soon ...... at her witty remarks.
The authorities must ...... decisive action against illegal dumping.
options: D be seen to be taking

items.sentence: Mr Harrison asked me to tell you that he would like ...... by noon at the latest.
options: D to have sent the reports

items.sentence: Taking up yoga ...... , but I wasn't particularly convinced.
There ...... between 6,000 and 7,000 languages spoken across the world today.
options: B have been said to be

items.sentence: ...she says she ...... while travelling through the capital.
options: B got her passport to be stolen
C had her passport be stolen

items.sentence: Why didn't you ...... you when you relocated to your new office?
Sales ...... considerably next quarter...
It's remarkable that this entire building ...... using only recycled materials.
As soon as Mum ...... , we can head to the restaurant.
options: B has got the car started
C will have the car starting
D gets the car to start

sections.title: J: Open Cloze (3D Printing)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Imagine (1) ...... able to manufacture a personalised prosthetic limb in a matter of hours rather than months.
Three-dimensional printers (2) ...... increasingly regarded as one of the most revolutionary tools available to modern medicine and industry.
This additive manufacturing process has long been (3) ...... development, with its origins traced back to early research in the 1980s.
In standard industrial production, material (4) ...... shaped by cutting, drilling, or moulding until the correct form is achieved.
...a digital design is loaded into a machine along (5) ...... the chosen material, and the object is built up layer by layer.
Each printer, which first has to (6) ...... calibrated precisely, deposits fine layers of material in exact accordance with the digital specifications.
It has (7) ...... calculated that the global market for 3D printing will be worth hundreds of billions of dollars by 2030.
Despite this promise, the reliability of the process (8) ...... not yet been fully established for high-stress industrial applications.
Although researchers (9) ...... successfully had 3D printers produce fully functional prosthetic limbs, achieving consistent outcomes at scale remains a significant challenge.
...and widespread commercial adoption may (10) ...... years away from being fully realised.
